perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Nevada, USA in force therein without regard to its conflict of law rules. All parties agree that by executing this Agreement they have attorned to the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of the United States. The Supreme Court shall have exclusive jurisdiction over this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Nevada, USA in force therein without regard to its conflict of law rules.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause specifying jurisdiction to the Supreme Court of the United States introduces ambiguity and potentially contradicts Nevada law, which outlines specific procedures for dispute resolution.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) Chapter 38 - Mediation and Arbitration
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada law provides a framework for arbitration agreements. By removing the clause that attorns to the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of the United States, the contract creates uncertainty about which Nevada laws apply for dispute resolution, thus creating a legal risk.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause specifying jurisdiction to the Supreme Court of the United States creates a potential legal issue, as it removes a clear designation for dispute resolution. Nevada law, as indicated by NRS Chapter 38 regarding mediation and arbitration, provides a framework for dispute resolution. While the original text explicitly stated the Supreme Court's jurisdiction, the changed text removes this specification, creating uncertainty. The removal of this clause could lead to disputes over which court has jurisdiction, potentially conflicting with the established procedures outlined in Nevada law for resolving disputes through arbitration or other means. The original text attorned to the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of the United States and the change removes this attornment, creating a legal loophole.

perturbation.original_text: Dolphin shall maintain at its principal place of business, or such other place as may be most convenient, separate accounts and records of all services provided to DGT, such accounts and records to be in sufficient detail to enable proper returns to be made under this Agreement. Dolphin shall deliver to DGT on the date 30 days after each and every year a detailed photo-editing report on all services provided on a unit basis (i.e. customer number, file number, cost).
perturbation.changed_text: Dolphin shall maintain at its principal place of business, or such other place as may be most convenient, separate accounts and records of all services provided to DGT.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for Dolphin to deliver a detailed photo-editing report annually potentially contradicts Nevada's requirements for maintaining business records.  While the agreement is for outsourcing, the lack of mandatory reporting removes DGT's ability to properly oversee its supply chain, which can affect regulatory compliance. Deleting the reporting requirement introduces a legal risk relating to business transparency.
perturbation.contradicted_law: NRS 86.241 Records: Maintenance; inspection; availability for examination.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 86.241 mandates that limited liability companies maintain records related to their business operations. While Dolphin is not directly mentioned as an LLC in the contract, removing the reporting requirements impacts DGT's ability to oversee compliance and indirectly affects its business operations within Nevada, creating a legal risk.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates Dolphin to deliver a detailed photo-editing report annually, while the changed text omits this requirement. According to NRS 86.241, limited liability companies must maintain records at their principal office to allow members and managers to examine records. The removal of the reporting requirement undermines DGT's ability to oversee compliance effectively because it removes a crucial mechanism for monitoring the services provided by Dolphin, creating a legal risk.

perturbation.original_text: DGT shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement if Dolphin fails to meet its requirements and material obligation hereunder and only after DGT have given Dolphin written notice of such failure and only after Dolphin has not rectified such failure within 10 days of the notice. Dolphin shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement only after DGT fails to meet a material obligation hereunder and only after Dolphin has given DGT written notice of such failure and only after the DGT has not rectified such failure within 30 days of the notice.
perturbation.changed_text: DGT shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement if Dolphin fails to meet its requirements and material obligation hereunder and only after DGT have given Dolphin written notice of such failure. Dolphin shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement only after DGT fails to meet a material obligation hereunder and only after Dolphin has given DGT written notice of such failure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the cure periods (10 days for Dolphin, 30 days for DGT) before termination could be seen as violating Nevada's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The omission creates a legal risk because Nevada courts require parties to act honestly and in good faith, which includes giving the breaching party an opportunity to cure the breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada law regarding the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although not codified as a statute, Nevada courts consistently recognize the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By removing the specified cure periods, the contract allows for immediate termination, potentially violating this covenant. The Restatement of Contracts § 205 and Nevada case law support the existence of this implied duty, which requires fair dealing and an opportunity to cure breaches when reasonable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the cure periods for breaches, which contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 addresses 'Obligation of Good Faith' within the Uniform Commercial Code, it broadly states that every contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. The removal of the cure period could be interpreted as a failure to act in good faith, thus impacting the enforcement of the contract. By omitting the cure period, the changed contract allows for immediate termination without providing an opportunity for the breaching party to rectify the failure, thus weakening the obligation of good faith. This omission creates a legal contradiction because it undermines fair dealing and the opportunity to cure breaches, essential elements of good faith as outlined in the explanations provided.
